title: Vaccine + Filler Panic! Does the Covid vaccine react with dermal fillers？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Vaccine + Filler Panic! Does the Covid vaccine react with dermal fillers？ [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: What's going to happen to every practitioner across the earth is in the next six months the vast majority of their patients are going to within that period of time have a reactive experience welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today …

segments.text: what's going to happen to every practitioner across the earth is in the next six months the vast majority of their patients are going to within that period of time have a reactive experience welcome to the aesthetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about a very important topic that you need to know a lot about it's vaccine and filler reactions you are going to be asked a lot of questions about your patients and you're going to be able to going to have to give really good advice about how to prepare for this given that we don't yet have all the answers but there's also something coming up towards the end which is going to help us get some answers i'm really excited about it so stay tuned until the end some people have been reacting some people who've had filler have been reacting to the covered vaccine do we know anything more about those reactions well just to slightly fine-tune what you said it's what's happening is a few people are getting facial swelling when they've had the vaccine it's not quite the same as saying that

there's an interaction between the vaccine and the filler but a lot of people do think that so they're thinking if you've had the vaccine there's some sort of interaction between the two and you're gonna have a almost like a drug reaction that's not what's going on and it's very important to understand that but there is basically this all came from one of the studies testing the modern vaccine which detected three people who had facial swelling around about the time of having the vaccine two of them had filler previously one six months before one two months before and one of them had filler the week after the vaccine and they all reported facial swelling so two were in cheeks i think one was in lips and all of them resolved with antihistamines and steroids and didn't require any reversal which is really positive do we know that's three people out of well good question the problem we have is there was no data collected on who'd had filler before having the procedure so of there about 13 000 people who were tested we don't know what population or how many of those had dermal filler in their system we only know about the three that reacted

three that reacted and that immediately makes the data not that useful and so we do have a lot of questions about it okay so what do these three poor reports of reactions mean for practitioners well the problem with social media and the media in general is that they will hyper focus on anything that's a potential threat this vaccine is such a hot topic there's a lot of uncertainty about it and for reasons that we're going to go into that i don't believe you need to worry um but we're going to talk about that later on the key thing is the patients are scared and if patients are scared they're going to look to their practitioners to bring certainty to them and you guys are going to have to do that so we need to be prepared for the questions you're going to be asked and when patients come asking for dermal filler for the next year because it's been going to go on for at least a year if not longer might be a yearly thing we have to have a vaccine we don't know yet take us back a step then this vaccine why are patients feeling uncertain about it and sort of putting that uncertainty onto the filler well firstly there's the background of how things that are scary get the most amount of attention and vaccines have

been basically there's a big focus a lot of people are anti-vaccines and you can always pull one half of an argument and expand it and make it as big and as scary as possible and that become makes it quite difficult for patients to know what the truth is you've actually got to go and look into it yourself and find out the the balanced view from both sides of it um but the fact is there's uncertainty and when there's uncertainty patients will ask their practitioners to give them certainty and that's what you guys have to do so we need to understand it from the point of view that there will be this is not like maybe back in the 80s when all vaccines were starting there wasn't really much question i think i just felt positive about them and my family just feeling positive about them because they've done incredible things and the situation is a bit different now it's particularly with social media and little pockets of people that get siphoned off to that create big stories out of very small fragments of the main story i think the reason that people feel uncertain about the vaccine is that we're all living this story right now it's been made in front of our

eyes and we kind of find it hard to believe that it's okay based on the fact that they've made it so quickly well that one of the big reasons people are doubtful is because it's been made so quickly so it is a record i think the fastest ever vaccine before this was uh the mumps vaccine i think it was made in four years so we've now done this in 12 months which is phenomenally fast it really is incredible but if you just look at that on its own it sounds like they must have cut corners you need to go looking at exactly what's different about this vaccine and the situation before you can actually understand that actually no corners have been cut that i can see when i've looked into this what i see is a really unique situation an absolute ton of resources there's never been orchestrated in such a way and basically a changing landscape in terms of how medicine is being done it's these genetic re recombination techniques rna vaccines they're very different to how they used to be that it's much easier to get the results plus the cooperation of the

international community which is also very unusual so how did they do it more quickly well the first thing is that the coronavirus itself has already been investigated in different forms i've been a couple of pandemics revolving around coronaviruses and they essentially have been preparing for this so the team who initially got this moving in america i think it's in texas they already were prepared and preparing for a rapid response to a vaccine and what they do is whenever there's a potential pandemic they start the process of producing vaccines and actually most of the time they get nowhere they've got very little funding proportionately this time they had the genetic sequence within a month of the first case being announced so the team in china shared the genetic code immediately so that immediately went around the world they already knew which protein to focus on so usually with when you're trying to find a virus and find trying to make a vaccine for it you don't really understand the virus that well for most for a lot of these vaccines like the hiv vaccine you it's different um sorry the hiv virus which you don't

have yet but with the coronavirus they knew that they were going to target the spike protein so they had the dna code for it and they actually did that within a few days like very quickly they could produce they could get the sec the sequence of rna that coded for the spike protein which they knew was going to be the vaccine so the big difference with this vaccine is it's an rna vaccine and they're not actually trying to harvest individual proteins and inject them into the cell they simply harvest getting the gna the dna code or the rna code for that particular protein then they inject that into your cell and that just reproduces so your cells actually produce the protein that your immune system learns about that then primes you to respond to it appropriately so it's different to other vaccines and in a way that's actually beautifully simple actually i think it's quite an amazing thing that you just inject the rna it doesn't go into the dna nucleus like a lot of viruses will actually embed their dna code into the nucleus of the cells into your dna the virus gets embedded well the rna viruses just take over the ribosomes and we'll we've got a really fun little

thing to show you about that but um this makes it very simple you inject the rna if you get the rna into the cell will make the protein for you the cell will make the protein for you just like it does with the virus but it's only one protein now a lot of the most controversial vaccines are attenuated vaccines so they're actually living viruses just half dead they basically just make them very weak and that gets a huge response from the immune system so they really work they do protect you from getting the full disease but they're just a bit more complex they're harder to produce take longer and have a few more risks associated with them so this is much simpler in many ways i think it's a simpler idea that you just get the cell to make it to make one tiny fragment of the cell and that's all that you need for your immune system to be trained up and you had it on friday didn't you which one did you have um i nearly said the left one yeah i had the fisa vaccine so in terms of people often ask you immediately after what was it like well it's a jab in the arm it doesn't hurt at all and i'm not actually amazing with needles but i have had my fair share and

then i had nothing except a tender arm for a while and i thought maybe i slept a bit more on the sunday but there wasn't a huge amount i haven't noticed anything so yeah pretty straightforward and easy so coming back to the filler the three filler reactions when people had the covered vaccine what does it mean for practitioners watching well as i've thought about this i've realized it could also it's basically a storm and a teacup but it is still a storm and what i mean by that is the chances of the of an individual patient reacting if they've had fellow to this vaccine is probably not that different to them reacting if they had a cold or a virus of any sort they may have the same inflammatory response the reason why it's still a storm is we've never been in a situation where all of your patients simultaneously within a short period of time all got colds that were significant enough to make them immune to a virus so effectively you what's going to happen to every practitioner across the earth is in the next six months the vast

majority of their patients are going to within that period of time have a reactive experience and even if the numbers are small you may find that you're dealing with more inflammatory reactions than you've ever had even though the risk to individual patients might be the same so that's quite hard i think a lot of people will miss that which is even if your risk as an individual is the same it's not the same for that practitioner who might have a list of you know what have we got do we got 12 the people we've treated over the last few years we might have 20 000 people who might suddenly have the vaccine and then call and say my filler is swelling that's possible not that twenty thousand of them but even if one percent or even point one percent of them it may be more than you've ever had before so this is why even though it's a storm and a teacup it's still a storm because you've never had so many people react at once so practitioners out there need to be prepared for it you need to know how to treat how to manage a delayed onset nodule a delayed reaction and you need to know how to communicate about it and reassure patients so it's that confidence going into this little storm

storm that i would hopefully we can contribute to helping practitioners with so how do vaccines work so as you know the kids have been off school because of lockdown i've involved them in a short little explanation of how vaccines work and i'm sure you're going to enjoy it so we're locked down now in the uk and i thought i'd use my kids to create this fun little analogy of how viruses and vaccines work which hopefully will help you and your patients jasper and xander are playing the role of the white blood cells looking for a tasty virus to eat viruses are made of several distinct molecules represented by this man built of different treats the white blood cells would love to eat so of course the problem with the coronavirus is that our immune systems do not recognize it to understand the vaccine let's review some basics let's do a quick recap on how cells normally work inside every cell you have your nucleus and inside the nucleus is dna is the code that runs everything it actually runs a lot of its functions through translating itself into rna and then from rna into protein

using these little cell factories called ribosomes literally print protein according to the code in the rna and the rna is made from dna that's happening all day long and is constantly printing out proteins according to the human genome these proteins are excreted often onto the cell surface and modified in all sorts of complex ways to make up a human body so let's have a look at how viral infection takes place viruses remember are made up of in the case of the coronavirus rna protein and lipid layers and they all form a structure that can replicate itself with the help of human cells but in order to do that they must take over the function of human cells so the way the coronavirus works is it has special receptors on certain human cells it'll effectively recognize those and then attach to the cell and introduce its rna into the cell now this is obviously an attack and there's a an overwhelming amount of rna injected

into the cell which means all those little ribosomes are usually making human proteins are taken over they can no longer make protein for the cell and they start to make copies of the viruses protein and sugars and you can see these coming out here as the next generation of viruses components are excreted by those ribosomes so those viral proteins which arrive on the surface of the cell in the case of the coronavirus they assemble on that surface and then they bud off and go off and repeat the process somewhere else infecting many other cells in the same way again the new coronavirus vaccine is actually really different to many of the vaccines that we've had in the past but actually all vaccines work in a very similar way that is they expose the immune system to some small component or version of the virus that is unable to replicate itself so attenuated vaccines are most of our childhood vaccines they actually use the whole organism and ex but they weaken it in such a way it's a bit like tying up our virus so it's the whole virus but it's unable to

move and operate properly that's one type of vaccine another type of vaccine is they take just components of the virus so they separate the proteins out take one of those proteins and inject them and that warns the immune system whenever it sees that protein no matter where it is to kill it now the new coronavirus vaccines are actually quite different in that they instead of injecting proteins they actually just inject a very short section of rna so picture that cell as it's getting infected there is reams and reams of rna that codes for an entire virus being squirted all over the inside of all of our cells what the scientists have done is taken just one section of that rna which codes for just one protein and they've put it into a little viral vector that enables us to inject just that piece of rna so the cells get the warning of what one protein looks like and that one protein is all they need to recognize the entire virus so we get the benefits of training the immune system without the risk of the

whole virus injecting its rna into our system so once the body has been vaccinated the mhc molecules will present the viral protein that has been made by the ribosome to the immune system in a special presentation dish called the mhc molecules the immune system will now immediately recognize anything with that protein as something it should eat and destroy so a vaccinated immune system now doesn't need any time to prepare for the invading force it is already prepared if it sees that virus it will devour it if i've had the vaccine and filler why might i react well put simply the vaccine is designed to create inflammation and when your body's in a pro-inflammatory state it basically all the symptoms that i've seen so far from the vaccine are just inflammation so one of the ones that hit the news quite early was bell's palsy that's probably caused by inflammation compressing the facial nerve so you get a neuropraxia

a neuropraxia um it's the same with the aches and pains the feeling tired you know the sore arm that's just inflammation it's all the same picture which is your body your immune system gets into action produces loads of inflammatory cells and filler just holds on to that inflammation a little bit you get some temporary inflammation most cases if you do nothing it resolves on its own but if you want to suppress it you can use antihistamines and steroids so if people are getting these three reactions did they do we know whether they reacted around the filler site or was it just they just generally got reaction and then it was attributed to the filler well they almost they certainly did get a general reaction like as in that's what the vaccines meant to do to give a general response now and then you pick up those signs like a slightly sore back some aches and pains some you know bells palsy in some people inflammation of your filler and other people and it's to do i think with the with a generalized hyperinflammatory state that reveals

reveals areas that are a bit more likely to become inflamed it's not quite the same thing as developing a reaction to filler because and i i'm only saying this because if you leave those people they usually it gets better on its own and they don't need any treatment so not for everyone i'm not saying that this is the right treatment i'm saying what i've noticed from talking to patients over the years is many of them will report that for a brief period of time on time of time they get some inflammation of their filler and if they don't do anything it goes away so that's very different from developing an autoimmune reaction to one of your organs or anything else like where it would persist it's not persistent it happens during the wave of inflammation and then it dies down and that is i think fundamentally different to a chronic reaction so some people might develop chronic reaction it's not one of the things that have been found out but if you have a really vicious reaction to filler it persists it carries on and on you need to get it out if you treat those same people again they react again that's very different to what you see with most pro-inflammatory states where

it's just temporarily inflamed treatable if you want to which speeds up the recovery and then it goes away it's not quite the same thing so just from my with my layperson words i as a patient would ask if my lips got more inflamed after the vaccine i would ask why is the vaccine attacking my lips yes well this is exactly it and it's really that's a really good question the vaccine is not attacking your lips the vaccine is doing what it's supposed to do it's causing an inflammatory response in your immune system so your immune system is hyperactive and that is revealing where your filler is because there's some reactivity around the filler it's your immune system being hyperactive it's the same thing as wise you know if you ask the question why is the vaccine attacking my joints because i feel aches and pains it's not that's your immune system it's why you get it with some viruses as well it's the immune system's reaction that causes all the symptoms but why don't i get a big lump in my forehead just randomly why is it attacking the

actual filler site well i do think there's something about filler that either holds on to moisture more or is pro-inflammatory there is an element of i know short-chain hyaluronic acids are a bit pro-inflammatory long-chain hyaluronic acids are anti-inflammatory that's one of the ideas that's around um and so that there might be something that's that is more inflammatory about fillers but it's not quite the same thing as developing an immune response specifically to the filler and i only say that because i know that if you do nothing most of them go away and you can also retreat them and they don't get another response and that's really different to being allergic to fellas in the other way that you can be okay so if people are having these essentially normal inflammatory responses that may or may not be um seem to be targeted at the filler you've got in your skin already after you've had the vaccine what should the practitioner do the first thing is actually if it's mild you might be better off not doing anything because you need that response to train

the immune system so it's paradoxical but you know although the patient might be concerned about it if it's mild and they're cosmetically not concerned it's just a little bit of puffiness you might just want to leave it for a bit longer you then if that's if it's bad enough that they're very upset then you could start to think about antihistamines and obviously if it's severe then steroids but there is a problem which is if you give patients steroids you may diminish the effect of the vaccine so you would ideally try and give them as long as possible without intervening i think give us a rough number of days hours well it's not something i would make in that way i would say um see the patient see what their response is you know if their face is swollen their eyes are shut like you need to give them steroids it doesn't matter they're probably getting enough of a response from the vaccine anyway if they're that swollen but there will be people who just have a little bit of swelling who are highly intolerant to just want everything neutralized who you should probably encourage to wait basically a full month like try not to do anything

to do anything especially the first seven days is very important you need that to give the immune system a chance to bed in um so i but like everything we do you do on a case-by-case basis have a look at the patient think about how severe the reaction is how they how it's affecting them psychos socially as well pull that into a decision-making process which is the medical model and decide if an intervention is warranted given their particular situation but one of your variables is the sooner you suppress it you may be suppressing the response to the vaccine and that's probably not going to be good for them long term so what's the likelihood of patients who've had the covered vaccine and they have filler in their skin what's the likelihood of them reacting well this is what we don't have the answer to and it's such a great opportunity for us to find it we can get this data in our study if you guys will help and hopefully we'll also find try and find out whether different fillers react differently because that would be fascinating to know anyway and one of the great things about not being kol is that we can research all these fillers

and report back to you and to tell you exactly what we find when patients come knocking on our door what are the things we need to consider so there are essentially four things we need to know for our patients you nee you need to know how the vaccine works because you have to have those kind of conversations you need to know why reactions happen the patient's going to ask you why on earth would this vaccine make my filler react because it's very it's one thing thinking it's just inflammation it's another if you think there's some sort of interaction between the vaccine and your particular filler it's a bit scarier so we need to be able to reassure them appropriately and we need to know how to treat these delayed onset reactions because um it's fairly simple but you need to know how to do it so that you can be confident in that situation and solve the problem and finally we need to know about what is the likelihood of this happening actually happening i think the most important unanswered question is we don't know what proportion of people have a vaccine are going to react we don't know which fillers might be a little bit more likely to react than others and this data is not very easy to get which is why i have a great idea

which i think all of you can help with so what do you need so i need your guys help because what i realize is we are a population of mainly medical aesthetic practitioners which means that you're going to get the vaccine first it's an amazing opportunity for us to gather data because i know many of you have had treatments and if you've had a treatment or even if you haven't had a treatment but you can fill in your experience of what happened we can look at this purely from the angle of medical aesthetics which is going to be so helpful for us to understand reactive lesions in general because never before we had so many people exposed to a known pro-inflammatory state and that you can study within a month of that actual incident happening you just can't do with viruses you don't know when the virus comes and when it goes we now will know this we can then try and get data about types of dermal filler that are involved in this reaction how they were treated so we can follow through hopefully an international population of patients having had the vaccine whether they react or not find out what the risk is and that's going to make it

so much easier to understand these situations in the future you're just going to have some really good data we'd love to make it global and we need your help you can even start your own little arm of the study where you can survey just your patients and then i'd love to come and talk to you about some of your results we'll pull all the results together and hopefully get it published should be an amazing study so remember if you'd like to be involved in this probably once in a lifetime kind of study because we're unlikely to vaccinate the whole planet like this again you can be involved in two ways number one you can send this survey to your list and see what you get back and then you have your own arm of the study you can see what the vaccines does in your particular population and i would love to speak to anyone who does that maybe we'll do a facebook live or an instagram live together and discuss those results but also you can just simply fill out the survey yourself so if you've had the vaccine or if you're not going to have the vaccine we actually need a control of people who've had dermatillo but haven't had the vaccine then please also get involved you can

also fill out the survey and just say that you haven't had it yet so click the link in the description and if you partake then we can send you the data and the results so that you can reassure your patients hopefully that will be the outcome when they are start asking you all the questions about the vaccine and filler like and subscribe to the youtube channel as well because then you'll also be presented with the updates as they come through take care bye
